level-1-taint-eviction.description: Running pods are being evicted unexpectedly from a node that just had a maintenance taint applied. The pod has no toleration for the taint — so when the `NoExecute` taint lands on the node, Kubernetes evicts it immediately with no grace window.
level-1-taint-eviction.hint1: Check the node's current taints: `kubectl describe node kind-control-plane | grep -A5 Taints`
level-1-taint-eviction.hint2: You should see a `NoExecute` taint like `maintenance=draining:NoExecute`. Look at the pod spec — it has no `tolerations` block at all. Without a toleration, a `NoExecute` taint causes instant eviction.
level-1-taint-eviction.hint3: The pod spec has no `tolerations`. Add a toleration matching the `maintenance=draining:NoExecute` taint and include `tolerationSeconds: 300` to get a 5-minute grace window before eviction.
level-1-taint-eviction.reveal:
## What went wrong

`NoExecute` taints behave differently from `NoSchedule`: they don't just block new pods — they immediately evict *running* pods that lack a matching toleration. No toleration = immediate eviction with no warning.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: maintenance
    operator: Equal
    value: draining
    effect: NoExecute
    tolerationSeconds: 300
```

`tolerationSeconds: 300` means the pod can stay on the node for 5 minutes after the taint is applied, giving it time to finish in-flight work before being evicted.

## Why this matters

The three taint effects have very different behaviors:
- `NoSchedule`: new pods without a toleration won't be placed here, but existing pods stay
- `PreferNoSchedule`: soft version of NoSchedule
- `NoExecute`: new pods blocked AND existing pods evicted immediately (unless `tolerationSeconds` is set)

Kubernetes automatically applies `NoExecute` taints for node conditions like `node.kubernetes.io/not-ready` and `node.kubernetes.io/unreachable` — which is why pods disappear when nodes go down. The built-in `tolerationSeconds: 300` default on most pods gives 5 minutes before eviction on node failure.

level-2-topology-spread.description: All pods from a 4-replica Deployment are landing on the same node. If that node fails, the entire service goes down simultaneously. There is no constraint telling the scheduler to spread pods across nodes — so it packs them wherever there is space.
level-2-topology-spread.hint1: Check where the pods are currently running: `kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l app=ha-app -o wide`
level-2-topology-spread.hint2: All 4 pods should show the same NODE. Look at the Deployment's pod spec — there are no `topologySpreadConstraints` defined. Without them, the scheduler is free to place all replicas on a single node.
level-2-topology-spread.hint3: The pod spec has no `topologySpreadConstraints` block. Add one with `maxSkew: 1` and `topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname` to enforce that no node has more than 1 extra pod compared to any other node.
level-2-topology-spread.reveal:
## What went wrong

Without scheduling constraints, the scheduler places pods wherever there is available capacity — often the same node. Four replicas on one node means one node failure takes down 100% of the service despite having 4 replicas.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      topologySpreadConstraints:
      - maxSkew: 1
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
        whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
        labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: ha-app
```

## Why this matters

`TopologySpreadConstraint` is the modern replacement for `podAntiAffinity` for pod distribution. `maxSkew: 1` means the difference in pod count between any two topology domains (nodes in this case) cannot exceed 1. For 4 pods across 2 nodes, that means 2 pods per node — a 50/50 split. `whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule` enforces this hard; use `ScheduleAnyway` for a soft preference. For zone-level spread (more relevant for production), use `topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone`.

level-3-pod-affinity-chicken.description: A new pod is stuck in Pending. It requires affinity to a pod with the label `tier: cache` — but no such pod exists in the cluster yet. The hard affinity rule creates a deadlock: the app pod can't start without the cache pod, but nothing is deploying the cache pod either.
level-3-pod-affinity-chicken.hint1: Check the pod's scheduling events: `kubectl describe pod app-pod -n k8smissions | grep -A10 Events`
level-3-pod-affinity-chicken.hint2: The events say 'didn't match pod affinity rules'. Look at the `affinity.podAffinity` block — it uses `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution`, which is a hard constraint. If no pod with `tier: cache` exists, this pod can never schedule.
level-3-pod-affinity-chicken.hint3: The `podAffinity` rule uses `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution`. Change it to `preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` so the pod can still schedule even if no cache pod is present yet.
level-3-pod-affinity-chicken.reveal:
## What went wrong

`requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` is a hard constraint: if the condition can't be met, the pod stays Pending forever. Requiring colocation with a pod that doesn't exist yet creates a chicken-and-egg deadlock.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              tier: cache
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
```

## Why this matters

The naming is long but the distinction matters:
- `required`: hard constraint — pod stays Pending if unmet
- `preferred`: soft constraint — scheduler tries to satisfy it but schedules anyway if it can't

Use `required` only when the placement constraint is a hard correctness requirement (e.g., a pod that literally cannot function without a local cache sidecar). Use `preferred` for performance optimization — scheduling near a cache pod is faster, but not necessary for correctness. When in doubt, start with `preferred` and only upgrade to `required` if you're sure the target pod will always exist first.

level-4-anti-affinity-block.description: A Deployment with 3 replicas has 2 pods running but the third is permanently Pending. The anti-affinity rule requires each pod to land on a unique node — but there are only 2 nodes in the cluster. The third pod has no valid node to go to.
level-4-anti-affinity-block.hint1: Check the Pending pod's events: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions -l app=distributed | grep -A10 Events`
level-4-anti-affinity-block.hint2: The events should say '3 node(s) didn't match pod anti-affinity rules'. Check how many nodes exist: `kubectl get nodes`. Then look at the anti-affinity rule type in the pod spec.
level-4-anti-affinity-block.hint3: The `podAntiAffinity` uses `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` with `topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname`. With 3 replicas and 2 nodes, the third pod can never satisfy this hard constraint. Change it to `preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution`.
level-4-anti-affinity-block.reveal:
## What went wrong

`required` anti-affinity with `topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname` means each pod must be on a different node. With 3 replicas and 2 nodes, the third pod violates the constraint on every available node — it can never schedule.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: distributed
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
```

## Why this matters

This is one of the most common scheduling mistakes in small clusters. Required anti-affinity with hostname topology is effectively a constraint that `replicas <= node count`. It works fine in a large cluster but permanently blocks pods in smaller environments. `preferred` anti-affinity gives you the spread you want when nodes are available, but falls back to co-scheduling when necessary. If you truly need one-pod-per-node for correctness (e.g., a DaemonSet-like workload), use a DaemonSet instead — which is designed for exactly this pattern.

level-5-priority-preemption.description: A critical workload pod is stuck Pending while lower-priority development pods consume all available CPU and memory. The critical pod is assigned the wrong PriorityClass — it has lower priority than the pods it needs to displace, so the scheduler never preempts them to make room.
level-5-priority-preemption.hint1: Check the critical pod's events and its current priority: `kubectl describe pod critical-workload -n k8smissions | grep -E 'Priority|Events' -A5`
level-5-priority-preemption.hint2: The pod is using `low-priority` class. Check what PriorityClasses exist: `kubectl get priorityclass`. The `critical-workload` pod should be using a high-priority class so the scheduler will preempt lower-priority pods to schedule it.
level-5-priority-preemption.hint3: The pod spec has `priorityClassName: low-priority`. Change it to `high-priority` so the scheduler knows this pod outranks the pods currently consuming resources and can preempt them.
level-5-priority-preemption.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod is labeled `critical-workload` in its name but assigned the `low-priority` PriorityClass. The scheduler only preempts lower-priority pods to make room for *higher*-priority ones. With the wrong class, the critical pod queues behind everything else.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  priorityClassName: high-priority
```

## Why this matters

Pod priority determines two things: scheduling queue order and preemption eligibility. A higher-priority pod that can't schedule will cause the scheduler to look for lower-priority pods to evict to make room. The numeric priority value (set in the PriorityClass) determines relative ordering — not the name. Common production tiers: `system-cluster-critical` (built-in, value 2000000000), `high-priority` (~1000), `default` (0), `low-priority` (~-100). Always use named PriorityClasses rather than raw numeric values — names are more auditable and refactorable.

level-6-spot-toleration.description: Batch pods running on spot/preemptible nodes are being reclaimed by the cloud provider. When the spot node disappears, the pods should reschedule to on-demand nodes — but they can't. A `nodeSelector` pins them exclusively to spot nodes, and they lack a toleration for the on-demand node taint.
level-6-spot-toleration.hint1: Check where pods are trying to reschedule after eviction: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions -l app=batch | grep -A10 Events`
level-6-spot-toleration.hint2: The events show 'node(s) had untolerated taint' or 'didn't match node selector'. Look at the pod spec — it has both a `nodeSelector: {node-type: spot}` and tolerations only for spot nodes. On-demand nodes have their own taint that isn't tolerated.
level-6-spot-toleration.hint3: Two things need fixing: the `nodeSelector: {node-type: spot}` blocks rescheduling to on-demand nodes, and there is no toleration for the `on-demand=true:NoSchedule` taint. Remove the nodeSelector and add the on-demand toleration.
level-6-spot-toleration.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod is doubly locked to spot nodes: the `nodeSelector` requires `node-type: spot`, and it only tolerates the spot taint. When spot nodes are reclaimed, the pod has nowhere to go — it can't reach on-demand nodes.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: spot
    operator: Equal
    value: 'true'
    effect: NoSchedule
  - key: on-demand
    operator: Equal
    value: 'true'
    effect: NoSchedule
  # Remove nodeSelector entirely
  containers:
  - name: batch
    ...
```

## Why this matters

Spot/preemptible nodes are cost-effective for batch workloads but can be reclaimed at any time. The resilient pattern: tolerate both spot and on-demand taints, but prefer spot using `nodeAffinity.preferredDuringScheduling` rather than hard-pinning with `nodeSelector`. This gives you spot pricing when available and automatic fallback to on-demand when reclaimed — no human intervention required. Never use `nodeSelector` for fault-tolerant workloads that need to survive node loss.

level-7-node-selector-miss.description: A GPU pod is stuck in Pending. The pod uses a `nodeSelector` to target nodes with a specific GPU label, but no node in the cluster has that label. The label was intended to be applied to a GPU node that hasn't been labeled yet — or the label key itself is wrong.
level-7-node-selector-miss.hint1: Check the pod's scheduling events: `kubectl describe pod gpu-pod -n k8smissions | grep -A5 Events`
level-7-node-selector-miss.hint2: The events say 'didn't match node selector'. Check what labels your nodes actually have: `kubectl get nodes --show-labels`. Compare against what the pod's `nodeSelector` requires.
level-7-node-selector-miss.hint3: The pod's `nodeSelector` has `accelerator-type: nvidia-v100` but no node has this label. Remove the `nodeSelector` block so the pod can schedule on any available node (or label a node correctly first).
level-7-node-selector-miss.reveal:
## What went wrong

`nodeSelector` is a hard constraint: the pod only schedules on nodes that have every key-value pair in the selector. The label `accelerator-type: nvidia-v100` doesn't exist on any node, so no valid placement exists and the pod stays Pending indefinitely.

## Fix

Remove the `nodeSelector` block:
```yaml
spec:
  # nodeSelector removed — schedules on any node
  containers:
  - name: trainer
    ...
```

Or label the correct node if GPU nodes exist:
```bash
kubectl label node <gpu-node-name> accelerator-type=nvidia-v100
```

## Why this matters

`nodeSelector` is the simplest form of node targeting — it's a map of required labels. For more flexibility, use `nodeAffinity` which supports operators (`In`, `NotIn`, `Exists`, `DoesNotExist`) and preferred vs. required semantics. Both `nodeSelector` and `nodeAffinity` are complementary: if both are specified, a node must satisfy both. Always verify node labels before writing selectors using `kubectl get nodes --show-labels`.

level-8-resource-fit.description: A pod is stuck in Pending despite the cluster appearing to have capacity. The resource request is the problem — the pod asks for 100Gi of memory, which exceeds what any single node can provide. Kubernetes must schedule a pod entirely on one node; it cannot split a pod across nodes.
level-8-resource-fit.hint1: Check the pod's scheduling events: `kubectl describe pod large-pod -n k8smissions | grep -A5 Events`
level-8-resource-fit.hint2: The events should say 'Insufficient memory' on all nodes. Check each node's allocatable memory: `kubectl describe node | grep -E 'Name:|memory'`. Compare against the pod's `resources.requests.memory`.
level-8-resource-fit.hint3: The pod's `resources.requests.memory` is set to `100Gi` which exceeds every node's allocatable memory. Reduce it to something that actually fits within the available node capacity — like `256Mi`.
level-8-resource-fit.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod requests `memory: 100Gi`. Kubernetes schedules a pod atomically on a single node — it cannot split resource usage across nodes (that's what distributed computing frameworks like Spark are for). No node has 100Gi allocatable memory, so the scheduler reports `Insufficient memory` on every node.

## Fix

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 256Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 200m
    memory: 512Mi
```

## Why this matters

Resource requests serve two purposes: scheduling placement (the scheduler sums requests to determine if a node has space) and QoS classification (requests vs. limits ratio determines whether a pod is Guaranteed, Burstable, or BestEffort). A request that exceeds any node's allocatable memory will keep the pod Pending forever — it's not a question of cluster size or adding nodes, because the pod needs 100Gi on a *single* node. Always size requests based on what the application actually needs at steady state, verified by profiling, not by theoretical maximums.

level-9-descheduler-config.description: One node is running 80% of all pods while other nodes sit mostly empty. The Descheduler is deployed to handle this kind of imbalance by evicting pods from overloaded nodes so they can reschedule to underutilized ones — but it's been configured with the rebalancing strategy disabled.
level-9-descheduler-config.hint1: Check the Descheduler policy configuration in the manifest and look for the `enabled` field: `kubectl get configmap -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A10 LowNodeUtilization`
level-9-descheduler-config.hint2: You should see `enabled: false` on the `LowNodeUtilization` strategy. This means the descheduler never acts, even though the policy thresholds are configured. The `enabled` field is the on/off switch for the entire strategy.
level-9-descheduler-config.hint3: The `LowNodeUtilization` strategy has `enabled: false`. Change it to `enabled: true` to allow the descheduler to evict pods from nodes above the `targetThresholds` and rebalance them to nodes below `thresholds`.
level-9-descheduler-config.reveal:
## What went wrong

The `LowNodeUtilization` strategy is configured with correct thresholds but `enabled: false`. The Descheduler reads this policy and skips the strategy entirely — no evictions happen and the imbalance persists.

## Fix

```yaml
apiVersion: policy/v1alpha1
kind: DeschedulerPolicy
strategies:
  LowNodeUtilization:
    enabled: true
    params:
      nodeResourceUtilizationThresholds:
        thresholds:
          cpu: 20
          memory: 20
          pods: 20
        targetThresholds:
          cpu: 50
          memory: 50
          pods: 50
```

## Why this matters

Kubernetes schedules pods at creation time but never moves them after placement. Over time, clusters become imbalanced: early pods fill the first nodes that had space; later pods pile on while other nodes sit empty. The Descheduler complements the scheduler by evicting pods from overloaded nodes (above `targetThresholds`) so the scheduler can replicate them on underutilized nodes (below `thresholds`). This improves availability (fewer pods per node means less blast radius per failure) and resource efficiency. The Descheduler typically runs as a CronJob every 5–10 minutes.

level-10-allocatable-vs-capacity.description: A pod requests 5Gi of memory but can't schedule even though the node shows 6Gi total capacity. The scheduler doesn't use the raw capacity number — it uses allocatable memory, which is capacity minus what the kubelet and system processes have reserved. The pod's request exceeds the allocatable amount.
level-10-allocatable-vs-capacity.hint1: Check the node's actual allocatable memory vs. its total capacity: `kubectl describe node kind-control-plane | grep -E 'Capacity|Allocatable' -A6`
level-10-allocatable-vs-capacity.hint2: You should see `Capacity.memory` (e.g., 6Gi) and `Allocatable.memory` (e.g., something smaller, like 5.5Gi or less after kubelet reserves). The pod's `requests.memory: 5Gi` may exceed the allocatable amount even if it's within capacity.
level-10-allocatable-vs-capacity.hint3: The pod's `resources.requests.memory` is set too high relative to the node's *allocatable* memory (not its total capacity). Reduce the request to a value comfortably within the allocatable amount — like `1Gi`.
level-10-allocatable-vs-capacity.reveal:
## What went wrong

A node with `Capacity.memory: 6Gi` has a lower `Allocatable.memory` because the kubelet reserves memory for itself and system processes (`--kube-reserved`, `--system-reserved`). If allocatable is 5.4Gi and the pod requests 5Gi, plus existing pod requests, there's not enough allocatable memory — even though the raw node capacity looks sufficient.

## Fix

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 1Gi
  limits:
    cpu: 200m
    memory: 2Gi
```

## Why this matters

Always size pod requests against `Allocatable`, not `Capacity`. The formula:
```
Allocatable = Capacity - kube-reserved - system-reserved - eviction-threshold
```
Check it with `kubectl describe node | grep Allocatable`. A common mistake: calculating "I need 90% of 6Gi so I'll request 5.4Gi" — but allocatable may already be 5.2Gi after reserves, leaving only 200Mi free. The scheduler also sums all existing pod requests against allocatable, so even a node with low actual utilization may show `Insufficient memory` if many pods have reserved capacity.

level-11-extended-resource.description: A pod requesting a custom FPGA device resource is stuck in Pending. The device plugin is running and correctly advertising available FPGAs — but the resource name in the pod's request doesn't match what the plugin registered. No node reports the requested resource name.
level-11-extended-resource.hint1: Check the pod's scheduling events: `kubectl describe pod fpga-pod -n k8smissions | grep -A5 Events`
level-11-extended-resource.hint2: The events say '0 nodes have sufficient my.company/fpga'. Check what extended resources are actually available on nodes: `kubectl describe node | grep -A20 'Allocatable'`. Look for any `*.io/fpga` or similar resources.
level-11-extended-resource.hint3: The pod requests `my.company/fpga: '1'` but the device plugin registered the resource as `company.io/fpga`. Extended resource names must match the device plugin's registration exactly — including the domain prefix.
level-11-extended-resource.reveal:
## What went wrong

The device plugin advertises `company.io/fpga` as an allocatable resource on each node. The pod requests `my.company/fpga` — a different name. The scheduler searches for nodes with `my.company/fpga` capacity, finds zero, and keeps the pod Pending.

## Fix

```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    company.io/fpga: '1'
```

## Why this matters

Extended resources (also called device plugin resources) follow the format `<vendor-domain>/<resource-name>`. They must:
1. Use a domain prefix with a `/` separator (e.g., `nvidia.com/gpu`, `intel.com/ioatdma`)
2. Be declared under `limits` only (not `requests` — the scheduler treats limits = requests for extended resources)
3. Use whole numbers (no millicores equivalent for GPUs)
4. Match exactly the string registered by the device plugin DaemonSet

Verify available extended resources with `kubectl describe node | grep -v 'kubernetes.io'` to filter out built-in resources.

level-12-scheduler-name.description: A machine learning workload pod is being scheduled by the default Kubernetes scheduler instead of the custom ML-aware scheduler deployed for these workloads. Without specifying which scheduler to use, every pod defaults to `default-scheduler` — bypassing all GPU-topology and ML-specific placement logic.
level-12-scheduler-name.hint1: Check which scheduler placed the pod: `kubectl describe pod ml-pod -n k8smissions | grep Scheduler`
level-12-scheduler-name.hint2: The pod was scheduled by `default-scheduler`. Verify that the ML-specific scheduler is running: `kubectl get pods -A | grep ml-scheduler`. The custom scheduler exists but the pod never asked for it.
level-12-scheduler-name.hint3: The pod spec has no `schedulerName` field. Without it, Kubernetes always uses `default-scheduler`. Add `schedulerName: ml-scheduler` to the pod spec to route it to the correct scheduler.
level-12-scheduler-name.reveal:
## What went wrong

Every pod is processed by exactly one scheduler. The default is `default-scheduler`. Custom schedulers (for GPU topology, ML workloads, etc.) are deployed alongside it and process only pods that explicitly request them via `schedulerName`.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  schedulerName: ml-scheduler
  containers:
  - name: trainer
    ...
```

## Why this matters

Kubernetes supports running multiple schedulers simultaneously. Each scheduler watches the API server for pods in its queue (pods with a matching `schedulerName` that have no `nodeName` assigned yet). Custom schedulers implement specialized placement logic: GPU-topology-aware schedulers place ML pods so GPUs can communicate via NVLink; gang schedulers ensure all pods of a distributed training job start simultaneously; deadline schedulers prioritize time-sensitive batch jobs. The pod must explicitly opt in via `schedulerName` — there is no automatic routing based on pod content.

level-13-pending-deadlock.description: All pods in the cluster are stuck in Pending — even tiny pods with minimal resource requests that should schedule immediately. Nodes have available capacity. This is not a resource problem. A misconfigured admission webhook is blocking every pod creation request cluster-wide.
level-13-pending-deadlock.hint1: Try to create any pod and observe the error: `kubectl apply -f ~/.k8smissions/sandbox/manifest.yaml`. Also check admission webhooks: `kubectl get validatingwebhookconfiguration`
level-13-pending-deadlock.hint2: Look at the webhook's `failurePolicy` field: `kubectl get validatingwebhookconfiguration strict-validator -o yaml | grep failurePolicy`. If the webhook service is unreachable and `failurePolicy: Fail`, every pod creation is rejected.
level-13-pending-deadlock.hint3: The webhook has `failurePolicy: Fail` and the webhook service is down. Change `failurePolicy` to `Ignore` so pod creation can proceed even when the webhook is unreachable.
level-13-pending-deadlock.reveal:
## What went wrong

`failurePolicy: Fail` means: if the webhook service is unreachable (timeout, service down, DNS failure), treat the admission request as a rejection. With the validator service down, 100% of pod CREATE requests fail cluster-wide — a full scheduling deadlock.

## Fix

```yaml
failurePolicy: Ignore
```

This allows pod creation to proceed even if the webhook is unreachable. Fix the underlying webhook service, then revert to `failurePolicy: Fail`.

## Why this matters

Admission webhooks sit in the critical path of every API request matching their rules. A webhook with `failurePolicy: Fail` is a cluster-wide single point of failure for all covered operations. Best practices:
- Use `failurePolicy: Ignore` for non-critical validation
- Deploy webhook services with multiple replicas and a PDB
- Use `namespaceSelector` or `objectSelector` to limit scope
- Set a short `timeoutSeconds` (3–5s) to fail fast
- Add a `objectSelector` exclusion for the `kube-system` namespace to prevent the webhook from blocking its own service pods

level-14-node-pressure.description: Pods are being unexpectedly evicted from a node that appears healthy — CPU utilization is low but memory pods keep disappearing. One pod is allocating nearly 1GB of memory using a `dd` command while declaring only 50Mi in its requests. This mismatch triggers the kubelet's eviction manager.
level-14-node-pressure.hint1: Check node conditions to confirm memory pressure: `kubectl describe node kind-control-plane | grep -A5 Conditions`
level-14-node-pressure.hint2: You should see `MemoryPressure: True`. Now find the pod causing it: `kubectl top pods -n k8smissions`. Look at the `memory-hog` pod's actual memory usage vs. its declared requests.
level-14-node-pressure.hint3: The `memory-hog` pod runs `dd if=/dev/zero bs=1M count=1000` which allocates ~1GB, but its `resources.requests.memory` is only `50Mi` and `limits.memory` is `2Gi`. Change the command to `sleep 3600` and reduce the memory limit to `128Mi`.
level-14-node-pressure.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod's command `dd if=/dev/zero bs=1M count=1000 | cat > /tmp/data` allocates approximately 1GB of memory. The declared `requests.memory: 50Mi` is far lower than actual usage — the pod was scheduled based on a 50Mi reservation but consumes 1GB. This pushes the node into `MemoryPressure` and the kubelet begins evicting other pods.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - sleep 3600
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: 10m
        memory: 50Mi
      limits:
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 128Mi
```

## Why this matters

Understating resource requests is a common cause of node pressure. The scheduler trusts the declared request for placement — but actual runtime usage can far exceed it. When real usage exceeds the node's available memory, the kubelet evicts pods in order of QoS: BestEffort first, then Burstable (pods where requests < limits), then Guaranteed. Set requests to reflect realistic steady-state usage, and set limits conservatively above that — the gap is your burst headroom.

level-15-init-container-block.description: The main application container never starts. The pod has been stuck in `Init` state for over an hour. An init container is polling a database host that doesn't exist — so the connectivity check loops forever, and the main container waits indefinitely for the init phase to complete.
level-15-init-container-block.hint1: Check the init container's logs to see what it's doing: `kubectl logs init-pod -n k8smissions -c db-wait`
level-15-init-container-block.hint2: The logs show repeated `nc` connection attempts to `non-existent-db-host:5432` with no success. The init container is designed to wait until the database is reachable — but the hostname doesn't resolve to anything.
level-15-init-container-block.hint3: The init container's command polls `non-existent-db-host` which doesn't exist. Change the command to `echo 'DB check skipped'; exit 0` to let the init phase complete successfully and unblock the main container.
level-15-init-container-block.reveal:
## What went wrong

The init container runs `until nc -z non-existent-db-host 5432; do sleep 5; done` — a loop that exits only when the TCP connection succeeds. The hostname `non-existent-db-host` never resolves, so the loop runs forever. The main container starts only after all init containers exit 0, so it waits indefinitely.

## Fix

```yaml
initContainers:
- name: db-wait
  image: busybox:1.36
  command:
  - /bin/sh
  - -c
  - echo 'DB check skipped - DBaaS mode'; exit 0
```

In production, replace `non-existent-db-host` with the real database Service name once it exists.

## Why this matters

Init containers are designed for exactly this pattern — waiting for a dependency before the main container starts. They run sequentially and must all exit 0 before the main container phase begins. Common uses: wait-for-db, run migrations, copy config files, download secrets. The failure mode is always the same: if the init condition is never met, the main container never starts, the pod stays in `Init:0/1` forever, and `kubectl logs` on the main container returns 'container has not been started'. Always log what you're checking so `kubectl logs -c <init-container-name>` gives diagnostic output.

level-16-resource-class.description: A pod using Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) is stuck in Pending. It references a ResourceClaim that can't be satisfied because the ResourceClass it names doesn't exist. Without the class, the DRA driver has no configuration to allocate the resource from.
level-16-resource-class.hint1: Check the pod's events and the ResourceClaim status: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions | grep -A5 Events` and `kubectl get resourceclaim my-fpga-claim -n k8smissions -o yaml`
level-16-resource-class.hint2: The ResourceClaim status should show it can't be allocated because `non-existent-fpga-class` doesn't exist. List available ResourceClasses: `kubectl get resourceclass`. You'll find none matching.
level-16-resource-class.hint3: The ResourceClaim references `resourceClassName: non-existent-fpga-class` but no such ResourceClass exists. Create a ResourceClass named `fpga-class` with the correct driver name, then update the ResourceClaim to reference it.
level-16-resource-class.reveal:
## What went wrong

The ResourceClaim references `non-existent-fpga-class` — a ResourceClass that was never created. Without a matching ResourceClass, the DRA controller cannot find a driver to handle the allocation request. The pod stays Pending because its ResourceClaim is unbound.

## Fix

Create the missing ResourceClass:
```yaml
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha2
kind: ResourceClass
metadata:
  name: fpga-class
driverName: fpga.example.com
```

Then update the ResourceClaim to reference it:
```yaml
spec:
  resourceClassName: fpga-class
```

## Why this matters

Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) is the next-generation replacement for device plugins (introduced in Kubernetes 1.26). The resource model: `ResourceClass` defines the type of resource and which driver manages it; `ResourceClaim` is a pod's request for a specific instance of that class; the DRA driver allocates and tracks individual device assignments. The three-object chain (ResourceClass → ResourceClaim → Pod) must be complete before the scheduler can place the pod. DRA enables more flexible structured parameters than the simple count-based model of device plugins.

level-17-balloon-pod.description: A node shows low CPU and memory *utilization* but new pods won't schedule — the scheduler reports `Insufficient cpu` and `Insufficient memory`. A balloon pod is running a no-op pause container but has declared enormous resource *requests*, silently consuming node scheduling capacity without using any real resources.
level-17-balloon-pod.hint1: Check total resource requests consumed on the node: `kubectl describe node kind-control-plane | grep -A20 'Allocated resources'`
level-17-balloon-pod.hint2: You should see most CPU and memory requests are consumed. Find which pod is responsible: `kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CPU:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu,MEM:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.memory`
level-17-balloon-pod.hint3: The `balloon-pod` has `resources.requests.cpu: 2000m` and `resources.requests.memory: 4Gi` on a `pause` container that consumes zero real resources. Reduce these to minimal values — `100m` CPU and `128Mi` memory — to free up scheduling capacity.
level-17-balloon-pod.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pause container (`registry.k8s.io/pause`) does nothing — it's a minimal binary that just sleeps. But its `requests.cpu: 2000m` and `requests.memory: 4Gi` tell the scheduler that 2 full CPUs and 4Gi of memory are reserved on this node. Real workloads can't schedule because from the scheduler's perspective, the capacity is gone.

## Fix

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 200m
    memory: 256Mi
```

## Why this matters

Balloon pods are a legitimate technique — they're used to pre-warm Cluster Autoscaler by keeping a node alive that would otherwise scale down, so it's available immediately for burst traffic. The key: the balloon pod should have a low PriorityClass and `cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict: 'true'`, so it gets preempted instantly when real work arrives. An oversized balloon defeats the purpose — it hogs the capacity instead of yielding it. Balloon requests should be small enough that real workloads can always preempt them.

level-18-scheduling-finale.description: Three teams are reporting scheduling problems simultaneously, each for a different reason. Team A's pod has a nodeSelector pointing to a nonexistent label. Team B's Deployment needs more unique nodes than exist. Team C's pod references a PriorityClass that was never created. All three are stuck Pending and must be diagnosed and fixed independently.
level-18-scheduling-finale.hint1: Check all Pending pods at once and read each one's events: `kubectl get pods -n k8smissions` then `kubectl describe pod team-a-pod -n k8smissions | grep -A5 Events` and repeat for team-b and team-c.
level-18-scheduling-finale.hint2: Each pod has a different failure reason: team-a sees 'didn't match node selector', team-b sees 'didn't match pod anti-affinity rules' (not enough nodes), team-c sees an API rejection for the unknown PriorityClass. Fix them independently — each has a different root cause.
level-18-scheduling-finale.hint3: Three fixes: (1) remove `nodeSelector: {team: team-a}` from team-a-pod since no node has that label; (2) change team-b-deploy's `requiredDuringScheduling` anti-affinity to `preferred` so 5 replicas can fit on 2 nodes; (3) remove `priorityClassName: non-existent-priority` from team-c-pod so it can be admitted.
level-18-scheduling-finale.reveal:
## What went wrong

Three independent scheduling failures:
1. **Team A**: `nodeSelector: {team: team-a}` — no node has this label, pod can never schedule
2. **Team B**: `required` anti-affinity per hostname with 5 replicas — needs 5 unique nodes but only 2 exist, 3 pods stuck Pending
3. **Team C**: `priorityClassName: non-existent-priority` — PriorityClass doesn't exist, API server rejects the pod before it enters the scheduler queue

## Fix

```yaml
# Team A: remove nodeSelector
spec:
  # nodeSelector removed
  containers: ...
---
# Team B: change to preferred anti-affinity
affinity:
  podAntiAffinity:
    preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
    - weight: 100
      podAffinityTerm:
        labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: team-b
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
---
# Team C: remove priorityClassName
spec:
  # priorityClassName removed
  containers: ...
```

## Why this matters

Real scheduling incidents often involve multiple teams with different failure modes active simultaneously. The diagnosis discipline: check each pod's events independently — `nodeSelector` failures say 'didn't match node selector', anti-affinity failures say 'didn't match pod anti-affinity rules', and PriorityClass failures are caught at admission (the pod may not even appear in `kubectl get pods`). Fixing one doesn't fix another — triage all three before touching the manifest, then apply all fixes at once.
